article.title: Deep-Ocean Archaea Slash Iron Needs by 80 Percent Under Warming, Rewriting Expectations for Marine Nutrient Cycling
article.summary: A study published in PNAS finds that the globally abundant marine microbe Nitrosopumilus maritimus dramatically reduces its iron requirements and boosts metabolic efficiency when ocean temperatures rise, suggesting deep-sea nitrogen cycling may intensify rather than collapse under climate change.
article.body_markdown:
## A Microbe That Defies the Forecast

As global ocean temperatures climb, scientists have broadly expected deep-sea ecosystems to suffer from compounding stresses: warmer water, lower oxygen, and declining supplies of essential trace metals such as iron. A [study published on March 11 in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2531032123) challenges part of that narrative, finding that one of the ocean's most abundant and ecologically important microorganisms does not merely survive warmer, iron-poor conditions but thrives in them.

The microbe in question is *Nitrosopumilus maritimus*, a species of ammonia-oxidizing archaea that, together with its close relatives, constitutes roughly 30 percent of all microbial plankton in the global ocean, according to the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](https://news.illinois.edu/new-study-finds-deep-ocean-microbes-already-prepared-to-tackle-climate-change/). These organisms drive a critical step in the marine nitrogen cycle by oxidizing ammonia into nitrite, a chemical transformation that regulates nutrient availability for virtually all other marine life.

## What the Researchers Found

The research team, co-led by Wei Qin of the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and David Hutchins, a global change biology professor at the University of Southern California, used trace-metal-clean laboratory experiments to expose pure cultures of *N. maritimus* strain SCM1 to varying combinations of temperature and iron availability, as described in the [PNAS paper](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2531032123).

The results were striking. A 5 degrees Celsius increase in growth temperature reduced the microbe's iron requirements by more than 80 percent. At the same time, the organism's iron use efficiency — a measure of how much carbon it can fix per unit of cellular iron per hour — increased substantially. In other words, under warmer conditions the archaea needed far less of a nutrient that is already scarce across vast stretches of the deep ocean and used what little they had far more effectively, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260311004708.htm).

The mechanism appears to involve optimized utilization of metalloenzymes, the iron-containing proteins that catalyze ammonia oxidation and electron transport within the cell. Rather than shutting down metabolic pathways when iron becomes limiting, the archaea restructure their biochemistry to extract more work from each iron atom, the [PNAS study](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2531032123) reports.

## Implications for the Global Ocean

To test what these laboratory findings could mean at planetary scale, the researchers incorporated their experimental data into a global ocean biogeochemical model. The modeling suggested that warming promotes nitrification at high latitudes, where conditions are shifting most rapidly, but that ocean circulation then redistributes the resulting changes in ammonia concentrations to lower latitudes, according to the [University of Illinois](https://news.illinois.edu/new-study-finds-deep-ocean-microbes-already-prepared-to-tackle-climate-change/).

The finding complicates existing climate models, many of which do not account for microbial metabolic plasticity when projecting how ocean nutrient cycles will respond to warming. If deep-ocean archaeal communities maintain or even intensify their role in nitrogen cycling, the downstream effects on marine primary production, carbon export, and the biological pump could be significant, the researchers noted in the [PNAS paper](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2531032123).

"Scientists previously assumed that warming and iron limitation would jointly stress these microbes," [EurekAlert reported](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1119333), summarizing the study's departure from prior expectations. Instead, the data suggest that deep-ocean archaea may already possess the metabolic toolkit to adapt to conditions that climate models project for the coming decades.

## What Comes Next

The laboratory results now face a real-world test. Qin and Hutchins will serve as co-chief scientists aboard the research vessel *Sikuliaq* during the summer of 2026, leading a team of 20 researchers on an expedition from Seattle through the Gulf of Alaska and south to the subtropical gyre near Honolulu, according to the [University of Illinois](https://news.illinois.edu/new-study-finds-deep-ocean-microbes-already-prepared-to-tackle-climate-change/). The cruise aims to validate the experimental findings by measuring archaeal iron use efficiency and nitrification rates across a natural gradient of temperature and iron availability in the Pacific.

Whether the microbes' adaptability translates seamlessly from controlled cultures to the complexity of open-ocean ecosystems remains an open question. The deep ocean hosts a web of competing organisms, fluctuating nutrient supplies, and physical mixing processes that laboratory conditions cannot fully replicate. The *Sikuliaq* expedition will provide the first field-level data to determine how closely the model predictions match observed conditions.

The study adds to a growing body of research suggesting that microbial responses to climate change may be more nuanced and, in some cases, more resilient than previously assumed. For a planet whose oceans absorb roughly a quarter of annual carbon dioxide emissions, understanding how their microscopic inhabitants will reshape nutrient cycling under warming conditions carries consequences that extend well beyond the deep sea.
